figures.gloss: The supreme creator of the Rapa Nui, born from a floating skull in the ocean. He fashioned humans from earth and his own semen, gradually usurping older Polynesian pantheons to become the sole patron and supreme deity of the fierce Birdman cult.
figures.facet: He represents a theological bottleneck. As ecological collapse forced a harsh new religious synthesis, Makemake ascended to absolute supremacy, absorbing the functions of the traditional Polynesian creator gods into a single, intense focus.
figures.provenance: Katherine Routledge (1919) 'The Mystery of Easter Island'; Alfred Métraux (1940) 'Ethnology of Easter Island'.

figures.gloss: A sacred earthly avatar chosen annually through a perilous, lethal race down the Rano Kau cliff and a swim through shark-infested waters to retrieve the first Manutara egg of the spring.
figures.facet: Transforms divine right from hereditary bloodlines to brutal, meritocratic physical competition. The victor did not just rule; he became the terrifying, taboo-shrouded vessel of Makemake, isolated in a ceremonial house for an entire year.
figures.provenance: Routledge (1919); Patrick McCoy (1979) 'The Place of Origins'.

figures.gloss: Colossal megalithic tuff statues representing deified paramount chiefs. Placed upon coastal ahu (ceremonial platforms), they channeled concentrated ancestral 'mana' to ensure agricultural fertility, fresh water, and tribal protection for their descendants.
figures.facet: Unlike most world monuments looking outward or upward to gods, the moai stand with their backs to the sea, projecting their gaze intensely inward upon human settlements—psychic batteries sustaining a closed, fragile ecology.
figures.provenance: Jo Anne Van Tilburg (1994) 'Easter Island: Archaeology, Ecology and Culture'.

figures.gloss: The legendary first paramount chief who arrived at Anakena beach in two double-hulled canoes after a cataclysm sank his homeland of Hiva. He brought the original crops, animals, and the foundational social order.
figures.facet: Not a mythical being but a venerated historical colonizer whose calculated, desperate arrival establishes the baseline of survival. He embodies the sheer will required to transplant an entire civilization onto a tiny speck of land.
figures.provenance: Manuscript of E. Te Rito (Barthel translations); Métraux (1940).

figures.gloss: Ethereal guardian spirits and ghosts of the deceased, famously depicted in wood carvings as emaciated, skeletal figures (moai kavakava). They possess intimate knowledge of the island's hidden resources and fiercely enforce social taboos.
figures.facet: Rather than dwelling in a distant underworld, they hyper-localize. Every cave, quarry, and family plot has its specific guardian, reflecting an island where space was intensely scarce and property boundaries were fiercely defended.
figures.provenance: Alfred Métraux, Ethnology of Easter Island (1940); Thor Heyerdahl, Aku-Aku (1958) (popular account, methodologically contested).

figures.gloss: A major deity of the Orongo ceremonial center, attested by Routledge as Makemake's constant companion and co-recipient of offerings — not a twin in the mythic-twin sense, but a paired secondary god named alongside him in ritual formulas. Fishermen and egg-hunters traditionally offered their sacrifices to 'Makemake and Haua' as an inseparable pair.
figures.facet: He functions as the essential dualistic anchor. While Makemake is the supreme creator, he requires Haua's active participation to receive ritual offerings, preventing the Rapa Nui theology from collapsing into absolute monotheism.

figures.gloss: The sacred sooty tern, a migratory seabird whose spring arrival signaled the return of life and triggered the intense Birdman competition. Securing its first uncracked egg was the ultimate prize of Orongo.
figures.facet: Embodies a theology of extreme scarcity. Salvation, cosmic renewal, and political stability are not guaranteed by celestial cycles, but hinge entirely on the precarious biological arrival of a single migrant bird.

figures.gloss: The 'speaking wood', an undeciphered system of glyphs carved into toromiro wood using shark teeth. Known only to the ruling elite, it was used by priests to chant genealogies, creation myths, and lunar calendars.
figures.facet: An incredibly rare independent invention of script born in total isolation. It sought to trap the volatile, oral mana of Polynesian chant into permanent, physical objects to withstand the chaotic breakdown of island society.
figures.provenance: Steven Roger Fischer (1997) 'Rongorongo: The Easter Island Script'.

figures.gloss: The god who, in Rapa Nui oral tradition recorded by Katherine Routledge and Alfred Métraux, used a great lever or pole to raise and sink lands one after another until, prying at Rapa Nui itself, his lever broke — leaving the island as the sole remnant above the sea. The story is linked to the lost homeland of Hiva, explaining why the ancestors' original land no longer exists to return to.
figures.facet: He supplies the tradition's own etiology for its extreme isolation — Rapa Nui's aloneness in the ocean is not simply geographic fact but the residue of a divine accident, the one land the god's tool failed to destroy.
figures.provenance: Katherine Routledge, The Mystery of Easter Island (1919); Alfred Métraux, Ethnology of Easter Island (1940).

figures.gloss: The homeland Hotu Matu'a's fleet abandoned or lost to catastrophe before its double-hulled canoes reached Rapa Nui. In afterlife beliefs recorded by early ethnographers, the spirits of the dead are said to travel back westward toward Hiva, making it both point of origin and destination of the dead.
figures.facet: It gives Rapa Nui cosmology a single directional axis — west is both ancestral departure and posthumous return — compressing origin and afterlife into one geography rather than treating them as separate realms.
figures.provenance: Manuscript of E. Te Rito (Barthel translations); Alfred Métraux, Ethnology of Easter Island (1940); Katherine Routledge, The Mystery of Easter Island (1919).

edges.note: Intra-tradition: The inseparable divine dyad receiving offerings at Orongo; the supreme creator requires a twin to interact with the ritual economy.
Intra-tradition: The distant creator and his violent, immediate, flesh-and-blood avatar on the island.
Intra-tradition: The sacred king whose entire divinity and authority derives from successfully capturing the bird's egg.
Intra-tradition: The monumental, collective manifestation of ancestral mana versus its intimate, skeletal, and mischievous counterpart.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Rapa Nui theology merged the ancient Polynesian sea god Tangaroa into the new, localized supreme figure of Makemake.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The creation of humanity from earth by Makemake directly adopts the pan-Polynesian myth of Tane.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both traditions select a human to become the terrifying, living embodiment of a deity for a strictly delimited period of time.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Extreme, potentially lethal physical athletic competition employed to resolve intense political and cosmic tensions among rival factions.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Pushing the human physical body to extreme exhaustion to cross the boundary into the spiritual realm and renew the community.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Venerated, highly formalized representations of ancestors who anchor the living community and guarantee its fertility.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Colossal stone pillars linking the earth to the divine ancestral realm, charging the ceremonial platforms with localized mana.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A legendary sovereign who organizes a desperate migration to save the seeds of civilization from a catastrophic destruction in the homeland.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Emaciated, fierce, and highly localized spirits that fiercely guard specific territorial boundaries and require constant propitiation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Ethereal ancestors who maintain acute interest in the moral and property-related affairs of their living descendants.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The retrieval of an uncracked egg serves as the literal mechanism for renewing the fractured world.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A highly esoteric system of elite priestly knowledge that anchored the cosmic state, entirely lost when the priestly class collapsed.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The conceptualization of carved script as an inherently magical force that binds and commands the universe.

The mana the moai concentrate and the tapu that shrouded them are the same inherited Polynesian sacred-power vocabulary carried to the mirror's farthest eastern shard — the words are tier-1 cognates; the concept-to-monument deployment is Rapa Nui's own.

GOLD SEAM: both islands preserve the East Polynesian founding-voyage charter — a named ancestral homeland (Hawaiki / Hiva), chiefly double-hulled canoes, crops carried aboard — the same inherited settlement-tradition genre, independently localized at the two far corners of the triangle.

Two founding-voyage discoverer-ancestors of the East Polynesian settlement corpus — Kupe naming Aotearoa's coasts, Hotu Matu'a landing at Rapa Nui — independently localized at the triangle's two far corners.
Intra-tradition: Uoke's lever-catastrophe is the myth-explanation for Hiva's submersion, the homeland the migrants fled.
Intra-tradition: Hiva is directly named in this roster's own Hotu Matu'a entry as the homeland his fleet fled.
Hiva is the Rapa Nui reflex of the same pan-Polynesian lost-homeland term-set as Hawaiki, the identical founding-voyage vocabulary independently localized at the triangle's two far corners.

dossier.essence: The traditional religion of the Rapa Nui, the Polynesian people of Easter Island in the southeastern Pacific, settled by roughly 1200 CE and devastated in the 19th century by slave raids, epidemics, and missionization. Scholars distinguish an earlier ancestor cult that raised the monumental moai on coastal platforms (ahu) from a later Birdman (Tangata Manu) cult centered at Orongo on the creator god Makemake. Both were charged with mana (ancestral potency) and regulated by tapu (sacred prohibition).
dossier.cosmos: Reality was permeated by mana and tapu; deified chiefly ancestors, embodied in the moai, watched over their descendants' lands and secured fertility. The creator Makemake, with his companion Haua, presided over the later cult, while guardian spirits and ghosts of the dead (aku-aku) attached to specific caves, families, and places. The annual return of the migratory sooty tern (manutara) signaled seasonal and cosmic renewal. The dead persisted as ancestral powers, their potency concentrated for chiefs in their carved stone images.
dossier.practice: The great collective labor was carving the moai at the Rano Raraku quarry, moving them, and erecting them on ahu with their gaze turned inland over the settlements. The later Birdman rite was an annual contest in which champions (hopu) representing rival chiefs descended the Rano Kau cliff, swam to the islet Motu Nui, and returned with the season's first tern egg; the winner's sponsor became the sacred, tapu-bound Tangata Manu for the year. Priests maintained tapu, and a literate elite used the undeciphered rongorongo glyphs to record chant and genealogy.
dossier.canon: No native scripture — an oral tradition of chant, genealogy, and myth, plus the undeciphered rongorongo inscriptions carved in wood. The record leans heavily on early-20th-century ethnography (Katherine Routledge, 1919; Alfred Métraux, 1940) and on the archaeological and monumental record.
dossier.reflects: Religion reorganized under absolute ecological limit — the sacred shifting from monumental ancestral permanence to an annual, competitive renewal riding on the arrival of a single migratory bird.
sources: Katherine Routledge, 'The Mystery of Easter Island' (1919)
Jo Anne Van Tilburg, 'Easter Island: Archaeology, Ecology and Culture' (1994)
Steven Roger Fischer, 'Rongorongo: The Easter Island Script' (1997)
